title: Exception Handling
description: Python exception handling from basics to Staff+ patterns: try/except/else/finally, exception chaining, context suppression, custom exceptions, and error handling architecture.

sections.title: 1. The Full try Block — else and finally
sections.description:
The complete try block has four clauses. Most engineers only know three.

try: code that may raise.
except: handle specific exceptions (always specify types, never bare except:).
else: runs if NO exception occurred — use for code that should NOT suppress the exception.
finally: ALWAYS runs — cleanup (close files, release locks).

FAANG angle: 'What's the difference between else and finally?' — else runs only on success, finally always runs even if an unhandled exception propagates.
sections.codeBlock:
def safe_divide(a, b):
    result = None
    try:
        result = a / b
    except ZeroDivisionError:
        print('cannot divide by zero')
        raise  # re-raise after logging
    except TypeError as e:
        print(f'type error: {e}')
        return None
    else:
        print('division succeeded')  # runs only if no exception
        return result
    finally:
        print('cleanup: always runs')  # even if return or raise in except

# Bare except is a linting error
# try:
#     risky()
# except:           # catches BaseException: KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit, etc.
#     pass

sections.title: 2. Custom Exceptions — When and How
sections.description:
Always inherit from Exception, not BaseException. Use a base exception per module/package for clean catching. Add context (status codes, retry hints) as attributes.

Best practice: MyAppError(BaseException) → MyAppError(Exception). Every module gets one exception class users can catch. The hierarchy should never be deeper than 3 levels.

FAANG angle: 'Design an exception hierarchy for an HTTP client' — base HTTPError, then ClientError(4xx) / ServerError(5xx) / TimeoutError / ConnectionError. Each carries status_code, headers, body.
sections.codeBlock:
class PaymentError(Exception):
    '''Base exception for payment module'''

class InsufficientFunds(PaymentError):
    def __init__(self, balance, amount, currency='USD'):
        self.balance = balance
        self.amount = amount
        self.currency = currency
        super().__init__(
            f'Insufficient funds: {balance} {currency} < {amount} {currency}'
        )

class FraudDetected(PaymentError):
    def __init__(self, transaction_id, reason):
        self.transaction_id = transaction_id
        self.reason = reason
        super().__init__(f'Fraud detected: {transaction_id} - {reason}')

class PaymentGatewayTimeout(PaymentError):
    def __init__(self, gateway, timeout_ms):
        self.gateway = gateway
        self.timeout_ms = timeout_ms
        super().__init__(f'{gateway} timed out after {timeout_ms}ms')

# Usage: catch the base, inspect the type
# try:
#     process_payment(100)
# except PaymentError as e:
#     if isinstance(e, InsufficientFunds):
#         prompt_for_topup(e.balance, e.amount)
#     elif isinstance(e, FraudDetected):
#         alert_security(e.transaction_id, e.reason)
#     else:
#         retry_with_backoff()

sections.title: 3. Exception Chaining and Suppression
sections.description:
raise ... from chains exceptions. The __cause__ attribute preserves the original traceback. Use raise ... from None to suppress the chain (when the new exception is the real cause, not a conversion).

Chaining is critical for debugging — without it, you lose context about what actually went wrong.

FAANG angle: 'You catch a ValueError and raise an APIError. How do you preserve the original traceback?' — raise APIError from e. The traceback shows both.

sections.title: 4. Exception Suppression in the Standard Library
sections.description:
contextlib.suppress: one-liner for silencing specific exceptions. Use it, not a try/pass block.

contextlib.redirect_stderr/stdout: capture output during shutdown or testing.

warnings.warn: for deprecated APIs — not errors, but signals for future migration. Convert to errors in tests with -W error.

FAANG angle: 'Your cleanup code always raises ResourceWarning. How do you suppress it?' — either contextlib.suppress(ResourceWarning) or warnings.filterwarnings('ignore', category=ResourceWarning) in the cleanup.
sections.codeBlock:
from contextlib import suppress
import os

# Instead of:
# try:
#     os.remove('temp.txt')
# except FileNotFoundError:
#     pass

with suppress(FileNotFoundError):
    os.remove('temp.txt')

# Multiple exceptions
with suppress(FileNotFoundError, PermissionError):
    os.remove('/protected/temp.txt')

# warnings to errors in tests
import warnings
# In pytest.ini: filterwarnings = error
warnings.warn('use new_api() instead', DeprecationWarning)

sections.title: 5. Exception Safety — Resource Cleanup Guarantees
sections.description:
Python guarantees finally and context managers run. Use context managers for ALL resources (files, locks, connections, transactions).

Resource acquisition is initialization (RAII): acquire in __enter__, release in __exit__. __exit__ receives the exception info — return True to suppress, False to propagate.

Thread safety: exceptions in one thread don't affect others. Use threading.Lock as a context manager. Never release a lock manually.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Design a retry wrapper that preserves the original exception' — use raise ... from e inside the loop to chain each failure, with the final exception showing the full retry chain.

sections.title: 6. Staff+ Exception Architecture
sections.description:
At scale, exception handling becomes an architectural concern, not a per-function choice.

Error boundaries (like React's): wrap entire subsystems (API handlers, workers, batch jobs) with a boundary that catches, logs, and converts to a standardized error response.

Structured logging of exceptions: include exception type, message, traceback (truncated), correlation ID, and context. Use logging.exception() inside except blocks.

Operational vs. programming errors: Operational (network, disk, timeout) are expected and recoverable. Programming (TypeError, AttributeError) are bugs — crash fast. Don't catch programming errors.

FAAG Staff+ angle: 'Design error handling for a distributed payment system' — operational errors retry with backoff, programming errors alert on-call, idempotency keys prevent double-processing, and error boundaries at each service layer ensure no crash takes down unrelated traffic.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Python Errors and Exceptions — Official tutorial
  • Built-in Exceptions — Reference
  • contextlib — suppress, redirect, closing utilities
  • Python Cookbook (3rd ed.) — Chapter 14: Testing, Debugging, and Exceptions

details:
Exception handling workflow: (1) Use specific exception types — never bare except:. (2) Use raise ... from to chain — never lose the original traceback. (3) Use context managers for all resource cleanup — never try/finally manually. (4) Design exception hierarchies for your module — one base per module, no deeper than 3 levels. (5) Log exceptions at the boundary, not in every function.

Common mistakes: Suppressing KeyboardInterrupt and SystemExit (bare except:); catching Exception at every call site instead of at the boundary; using raise e instead of raise (resets the traceback); catching programming errors and returning error codes instead of crashing.

Staff+ expectations: Design the error handling architecture for the service. Distinguish operational vs. programming errors. Build error boundaries. Ensure exception logging provides enough context for on-call debugging without leaking sensitive data.
